expanded_description:
Globalization—the increasing integration of economies through trade, investment, capital flows, migration, and technology transfer—is one of the most contentious issues in economics and public policy. Supporters argue that globalization raises living standards, reduces poverty, spreads technology, and promotes peace. Critics argue that globalization benefits the rich at the expense of the poor, destroys jobs in developed countries, exploits workers in developing countries, harms the environment, and erodes cultural diversity. The debate has intensified with the rise of populism, trade wars, and the COVID-19 pandemic, which disrupted global supply chains and led to calls for deglobalization (reshoring, protectionism).

Understanding the globalization debate is essential for evaluating trade agreements (USMCA, CETA, CPTPP), assessing the impact of offshoring and automation, and designing policies to manage the costs and benefits of global integration. The evidence shows that globalization has lifted hundreds of millions out of poverty (especially in China and India) but has also contributed to rising inequality in developed countries (manufacturing job losses). The winners and losers from globalization are not evenly distributed; policies such as adjustment assistance, education, and social safety nets can help redistribute the gains.

In this section, we will examine the benefits of globalization (growth, poverty reduction, technology, peace) and the costs (inequality, job displacement, exploitation, environment, culture). We will analyze the evidence on trade and growth, the role of multinational corporations, the impact of offshoring on developed country workers, and the policy responses to manage globalization. You will learn to evaluate competing claims about globalization, understand the distributional effects of trade, and assess proposals for reform (fair trade, labor and environmental standards, capital controls).

core_concept.principle:
Globalization—the integration of economies through trade, investment, capital flows, migration, and technology—has generated significant benefits but also significant costs. The net effect is positive, but the gains are unevenly distributed, creating winners and losers.

Benefits of Globalization:

• Economic growth: Trade liberalization boosts growth (empirical evidence: countries that open to trade grow faster).
• Poverty reduction: Globalization has lifted hundreds of millions out of poverty (China, India, Vietnam).
• Lower prices: Imports reduce consumer prices (especially for food, clothing, electronics).
• More variety: Consumers have access to goods from around the world.
• Technology transfer: FDI brings new technologies to developing countries.
• Economies of scale: Larger markets allow firms to achieve lower average costs.
• Peace: Countries that trade with each other are less likely to go to war (liberal peace theory).

Costs of Globalization:

• Inequality: Globalization has increased inequality within many countries (winners and losers).
• Job displacement: Import competition destroys jobs in developed countries (manufacturing).
• Worker exploitation: Sweatshops, low wages, poor working conditions in developing countries.
• Environmental damage: Increased production and transport lead to pollution, carbon emissions.
• Cultural homogenization: Americanization, loss of local cultures, languages.
• Financial instability: Capital flows can cause crises (Asian financial crisis 1997).
• Pandemic risk: Global supply chains spread disease (COVID-19).

Winners and Losers:

• Winners: Consumers (lower prices), skilled workers in developed countries, unskilled workers in developing countries, multinational corporations, capital owners.
• Losers: Unskilled workers in developed countries (manufacturing job losses), some unskilled workers in developing countries (displaced by imports), workers in sectors exposed to competition.

Evidence on Globalization:

• Poverty: Global poverty rate fell from 35% in 1990 to 9% in 2020 (World Bank). China's poverty rate fell from 88% to near zero.
• Inequality: Global inequality (between countries) has fallen; within-country inequality has risen in many countries (US, UK, China).
• Trade and growth: Countries that opened to trade (China, India, Vietnam) grew rapidly; countries that remained closed (North Korea) stagnated.
• Job displacement: US manufacturing employment fell from 17 million in 2000 to 12 million in 2020, partly due to trade (especially with China).

Policy Responses:

• Adjustment assistance: Retraining, unemployment benefits, wage insurance for displaced workers.
• Education and training: Help workers acquire skills for the new economy.
• Progressive taxation: Redistribute gains from globalization to compensate losers.
• Labor and environmental standards: Include in trade agreements (USMCA, CETA).
• Capital controls: Manage volatile capital flows.
• Social safety nets: Unemployment insurance, health care, pensions.

Deglobalization?

• The COVID-19 pandemic exposed vulnerabilities in global supply chains (masks, ventilators, semiconductors).
• Some countries are reshoring production (US, Europe).
• Trade wars (US-China) have increased tariffs.
• However, deglobalization is costly; most economists still favor open trade with better redistribution.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine a factory worker in Ohio who loses his job because the factory moved to China. He is a loser from globalization. Now imagine a factory worker in Guangdong, China, who gets a job at that factory. She is a winner. A consumer in Ohio buys a cheaper TV because it was made in China. She is a winner. A multinational corporation earns higher profits. It is a winner. The net effect is positive: total global output is higher. But the gains are not evenly distributed.

Globalization has lifted more people out of poverty than any other force in history. In 1990, 35% of the world's population lived in extreme poverty (less than \$2.15 per day). By 2020, that share had fallen to 9%. China alone lifted 800 million people out of poverty. India lifted 300 million. This is the triumph of globalization.

But globalization has also created losers. In the US and Europe, manufacturing employment has fallen sharply. Some workers have not found new jobs; others have taken lower-paying service jobs. Wages for workers without a college degree have stagnated. Inequality has risen. This has fueled populism, protectionism, and backlash against globalization.

The debate is not about whether globalization is good or bad on average. The evidence is clear: globalization has been a net positive. The debate is about how to manage the costs and distribute the gains. Should we have trade agreements with labor and environmental standards? Should we provide adjustment assistance to displaced workers? Should we tax the winners to compensate the losers? These are policy questions, not ideological ones.

For Canada, globalization has been a net benefit. Trade with the US (USMCA) is vital for the Canadian economy. However, Canada has also experienced manufacturing job losses (especially in Ontario). Canadian policy has responded with adjustment assistance (retraining, EI) and progressive taxation to redistribute gains. The debate continues over new trade agreements (CETA with EU, CPTPP with Pacific Rim countries).

image.alt: Diagram: Benefits (growth, poverty reduction, lower prices, technology, peace) vs. Costs (inequality, job loss, exploitation, environment, culture) of globalization.
image.caption: Figure 14.4.1: The globalization debate: benefits vs. costs. The net effect is positive, but the gains are unevenly distributed, creating winners and losers.

sections.introduction:
Globalization—the increasing integration of economies through trade, investment, capital flows, migration, and technology transfer—is one of the most contentious issues in economics and public policy. Supporters argue that globalization raises living standards, reduces poverty, spreads technology, and promotes peace. Critics argue that globalization benefits the rich at the expense of the poor, destroys jobs in developed countries, exploits workers in developing countries, harms the environment, and erodes cultural diversity. The debate has intensified with the rise of populism, trade wars, and the COVID-19 pandemic, which disrupted global supply chains and led to calls for deglobalization (reshoring, protectionism).

Understanding the globalization debate is essential for evaluating trade agreements (USMCA, CETA, CPTPP), assessing the impact of offshoring and automation, and designing policies to manage the costs and benefits of global integration. The evidence shows that globalization has lifted hundreds of millions out of poverty (especially in China and India) but has also contributed to rising inequality in developed countries (manufacturing job losses). The winners and losers from globalization are not evenly distributed; policies such as adjustment assistance, education, and social safety nets can help redistribute the gains.

In this section, we will examine the benefits of globalization (growth, poverty reduction, technology, peace) and the costs (inequality, job displacement, exploitation, environment, culture). We will analyze the evidence on trade and growth, the role of multinational corporations, the impact of offshoring on developed country workers, and the policy responses to manage globalization.

introduction_image.alt: Graph: Global poverty rate (1990-2020) falling from 35% to 9%; China and India accounted for most of the reduction.
introduction_image.caption: Figure 14.4.2: Globalization has been associated with dramatic poverty reduction. The global poverty rate fell from 35% in 1990 to 9% in 2020, with China and India leading the way.

key_concept.definition:
Globalization: The increasing integration of economies through trade, investment, capital flows, migration, and technology transfer.

Benefits of Globalization:

• Economic growth: Trade liberalization boosts growth (empirical evidence).
• Poverty reduction: Hundreds of millions lifted out of poverty (China, India).
• Lower prices: Imports reduce consumer prices (food, clothing, electronics).
• More variety: Consumers have access to global goods.
• Technology transfer: FDI brings new technologies to developing countries.
• Economies of scale: Larger markets allow lower average costs.
• Peace: Trade reduces likelihood of war (liberal peace theory).

Costs of Globalization:

• Inequality: Increased inequality within many countries (winners and losers).
• Job displacement: Import competition destroys jobs in developed countries.
• Worker exploitation: Sweatshops, low wages, poor working conditions.
• Environmental damage: Pollution, carbon emissions from production and transport.
• Cultural homogenization: Americanization, loss of local cultures.
• Financial instability: Capital flows cause crises (Asian financial crisis 1997).
• Pandemic risk: Global supply chains spread disease (COVID-19).

Winners and Losers:

• Winners: Consumers (lower prices), skilled workers in developed countries, unskilled workers in developing countries, multinational corporations, capital owners.
• Losers: Unskilled workers in developed countries (manufacturing job losses), some unskilled workers in developing countries (displaced by imports), workers in exposed sectors.

Evidence:

• Poverty: Global poverty rate 35% (1990) → 9% (2020).
• Inequality: Global between-country inequality fell; within-country inequality rose (US, UK, China).
• Trade and growth: Open economies grow faster.
• Job displacement: US manufacturing employment fell from 17M to 12M (2000-2020).

Policy Responses:

• Adjustment assistance: Retraining, unemployment benefits, wage insurance.
• Education and training: Skills for the new economy.
• Progressive taxation: Redistribute gains to compensate losers.
• Labor and environmental standards: Include in trade agreements.
• Capital controls: Manage volatile capital flows.
• Social safety nets: Unemployment insurance, health care, pensions.

Deglobalization?

• COVID-19 exposed supply chain vulnerabilities.
• Reshoring of production (US, Europe).
• Trade wars (US-China) increased tariffs.
• Most economists favor open trade with better redistribution.
key_concept.context: Globalization accelerated after WWII with the creation of GATT (1947) and later the WTO (1995). The fall of the Soviet Union (1991) integrated former communist countries into the global economy. China joined the WTO in 2001, accelerating its integration. The 2008 financial crisis and the 2016 election of Donald Trump (with his protectionist platform) marked a turning point. The COVID-19 pandemic (2020-2022) exposed supply chain vulnerabilities and led to calls for reshoring. The debate between free trade and protectionism remains central to economic policy. For Canada, globalization is especially important given the country's reliance on trade (30% of GDP).

image.alt: Diagram: Winners from globalization (consumers, skilled workers, developing country workers, MNCs, capital owners) and losers (unskilled workers in developed countries, some developing country workers).
image.caption: Figure 14.4.3: Winners and losers from globalization. The gains are not evenly distributed; policies are needed to compensate losers.

classifications.value: Globalization has raised living standards, reduced poverty, lowered prices, spread technology, and promoted peace
characteristics: Economic growth: Trade liberalization boosts growth (Sachs-Warner, Frankel-Romer).
Poverty reduction: Global poverty rate fell from 35% to 9% (1990-2020).
Lower prices: Imports reduce consumer prices (estimates: 1-2% of GDP).
More variety: Consumers have access to goods from around the world.
Technology transfer: FDI brings new technologies to developing countries.
Economies of scale: Larger markets allow lower average costs.
Peace: Countries that trade are less likely to go to war (Oneal-Russett).
classifications.behavior:
The benefits of globalization are substantial and well-documented. Countries that opened to trade (China, India, Vietnam) grew rapidly; countries that remained closed (North Korea, Cuba) stagnated. The global poverty rate fell from 35% in 1990 to 9% in 2020, with China alone lifting 800 million people out of poverty. This is the greatest poverty reduction in human history.

Consumers benefit from lower prices. A study found that trade with China lowered US consumer prices by 1-2% of GDP (about \$300 billion per year). Poor households benefit disproportionately because they spend a larger share of their income on traded goods (food, clothing, electronics).

Technology transfer occurs when multinational corporations bring new technologies to developing countries. Workers learn new skills; local firms adopt new methods. This has been a major driver of growth in China, India, and Vietnam.

Economies of scale allow firms to produce at lower average cost when they can sell to global markets. This benefits consumers through lower prices and benefits firms through higher profits.

The liberal peace theory holds that countries that trade with each other are less likely to go to war. Empirical evidence supports this: trade reduces conflict, though the relationship is not deterministic (WWI happened despite high trade).

image.alt: Scatter plot: Trade openness (exports+imports/GDP) vs. GDP per capita growth; positive correlation.
image.caption: Figure 14.4.4: Countries that are more open to trade tend to grow faster. This correlation is robust to controlling for other factors.

examples.explanation: China joined the WTO in 2001. Exports grew from \$250 billion to \$2.5 trillion by 2020. GDP per capita rose from \$1,000 to \$12,000. Poverty fell from 88% to near zero. This is the most dramatic example of the benefits of globalization.

Vietnam opened to trade in the 1990s and joined the WTO in 2007. Exports grew rapidly (garments, electronics). GDP per capita rose from \$200 to \$3,700. Poverty fell from 60% to 5%. Vietnam is a success story of globalization.

classifications.value: Globalization has increased inequality, displaced jobs, exploited workers, damaged the environment, and eroded cultures
characteristics: Inequality: Within-country inequality has risen in many countries (US, UK, China, India).
Job displacement: US manufacturing employment fell from 17M to 12M (2000-2020).
Worker exploitation: Sweatshops, low wages, poor working conditions (Bangladesh, Vietnam).
Environmental damage: Increased production and transport lead to pollution, carbon emissions.
Cultural homogenization: Americanization, loss of local cultures, languages.
Financial instability: Capital flows cause crises (Asian financial crisis 1997, Latin American debt crisis).
Pandemic risk: Global supply chains spread disease (COVID-19).
classifications.behavior:
The costs of globalization are also substantial. Inequality has risen in many countries, especially in the US and UK. The top 1% have captured most of the gains from globalization; the bottom 50% have seen little gain. This has fueled populism and backlash against globalization.

Job displacement in developed countries has been severe. US manufacturing employment fell from 17 million in 2000 to 12 million in 2020. While automation also played a role, trade with China was a major factor (Autor, Dorn, Hanson, 2013). Workers who lost manufacturing jobs often moved to lower-paying service jobs or dropped out of the labor force.

Worker exploitation in developing countries is a concern. Sweatshops in Bangladesh, Vietnam, and Honduras pay low wages (\$2-3 per day) and have poor working conditions (unsafe buildings, long hours, child labor). However, these jobs are often better than the alternatives (subsistence farming, informal work). The debate is whether sweatshops are exploitative or a stepping stone to development.

Environmental damage from globalization is significant. Shipping goods around the world burns fossil fuels, contributing to climate change. Production in countries with weak environmental regulations leads to pollution. The carbon footprint of global supply chains is large.

Cultural homogenization is a concern for many. American brands (McDonald's, Starbucks, Nike) have spread around the world, displacing local cultures. Languages are disappearing. Some argue that this is a loss of diversity; others argue that it is a natural result of consumer choice.

image.alt: Graph: US manufacturing employment (1970-2024); sharp decline after 2000, coinciding with China's WTO accession.
image.caption: Figure 14.4.5: US manufacturing employment fell sharply after China joined the WTO in 2001. Trade with China contributed to job losses, though automation also played a role.

examples.explanation: From 2000 to 2020, US manufacturing employment fell from 17 million to 12 million. Autor, Dorn, and Hanson (2013) found that trade with China accounted for about 25% of this decline. Workers in communities exposed to Chinese imports experienced lower wages, higher unemployment, and increased disability claims.

Bangladesh is a major exporter of garments. Workers earn \$2-3 per day, work long hours, and face unsafe conditions (the 2013 Rana Plaza collapse killed 1,100 workers). Critics call this exploitation. Supporters note that these jobs are better than the alternatives (subsistence farming, begging) and that wages have risen over time. This is the sweatshop debate.

classifications.value: Policies can help manage the costs of globalization and redistribute the gains
characteristics: Adjustment assistance: Retraining, unemployment benefits, wage insurance for displaced workers.
Education and training: Help workers acquire skills for the new economy.
Progressive taxation: Redistribute gains from globalization to compensate losers.
Labor and environmental standards: Include in trade agreements (USMCA, CETA).
Capital controls: Manage volatile capital flows.
Social safety nets: Unemployment insurance, health care, pensions.
Trade adjustment assistance (TAA): US program to help workers displaced by trade.
classifications.behavior:
The fact that globalization creates winners and losers does not mean we should stop globalization. It means we need policies to compensate the losers and help them adjust. This is the standard economic response: the gains from trade are large enough to compensate the losers, but compensation is not automatic; it requires policy.

Adjustment assistance includes retraining programs (learning new skills), unemployment benefits (income support during job search), relocation assistance (moving to areas with jobs), and wage insurance (compensating workers who take lower-paying jobs). The US Trade Adjustment Assistance (TAA) program provides these benefits, but it is underfunded and underutilized.

Education and training are essential for helping workers adapt to the new economy. Workers with college degrees have benefited from globalization; workers without have lost. Expanding access to education and training can help workers acquire the skills needed for modern jobs.

Progressive taxation can redistribute the gains from globalization. The winners (multinational corporations, skilled workers, capital owners) can be taxed to fund benefits for the losers (unskilled workers, displaced workers). However, globalization has also made it easier for corporations to shift profits to low-tax jurisdictions, complicating taxation.

Trade agreements increasingly include labor and environmental standards. USMCA includes provisions on labor rights (Mexico) and the environment. CETA (Canada-EU) includes similar provisions. This is an attempt to raise standards globally, not just protect domestic workers.

Capital controls can help manage volatile capital flows that cause financial crises. Malaysia used capital controls during the Asian financial crisis (1997) and recovered faster than its neighbors. However, capital controls are controversial and can deter investment.

For Canada, policies include Employment Insurance (EI) for displaced workers, retraining programs, and a progressive tax system. Canada also includes labor and environmental standards in its trade agreements.

image.alt: Graph: TAA benefits per worker (\$1,500) vs. wage loss (\$10,000); TAA covers only a fraction of losses.
image.caption: Figure 14.4.6: Trade Adjustment Assistance (TAA) provides benefits to workers displaced by trade, but the benefits are often inadequate to compensate for wage losses.

examples.explanation: The TAA program provides retraining, income support, and wage insurance to workers who lose their jobs due to trade. However, the program is underfunded and reaches only a fraction of displaced workers. Benefits average about \$1,500 per worker, far less than the \$10,000 average wage loss. Critics argue that TAA is inadequate; supporters argue that it is better than nothing.

Germany's Kurzarbeit program subsidizes reduced work hours instead of layoffs. During the 2008 crisis and COVID-19 pandemic, Kurzarbeit kept workers employed, preserving skills and reducing unemployment. This is an alternative to adjustment assistance that focuses on retaining workers rather than retraining them.

principles.principle: Globalization Has Reduced Global Poverty Dramatically but Increased Inequality Within Many Countries
principles.statement: The most important fact about globalization is that it has lifted hundreds of millions out of poverty. The global poverty rate fell from 35% in 1990 to 9% in 2020. China alone lifted 800 million people out of poverty; India lifted 300 million. This is the greatest poverty reduction in human history. However, within many countries, inequality has risen. In the US, the top 1% have captured most of the gains; the bottom 50% have seen little gain. In China, inequality has also risen sharply (Gini coefficient from 0.3 to 0.5). This pattern—falling between-country inequality but rising within-country inequality—is a key feature of globalization. The policy challenge is to manage the within-country inequality while preserving the benefits of globalization.
principles.application: Policymakers should not reject globalization because of rising inequality. Instead, they should implement policies to redistribute the gains: progressive taxation, social safety nets, education, and adjustment assistance. The goal is to make globalization inclusive. For Canada, this means maintaining an open trade policy while strengthening social programs to support those who lose from trade.
principles.example: China's poverty rate fell from 88% in 1981 to near zero today, but China's Gini coefficient rose from 0.3 to 0.5. The Chinese government has responded with poverty alleviation programs, rural development, and social insurance. The lesson: globalization can reduce poverty while increasing inequality; policy can address the inequality.

image.alt: Graph: Global poverty rate (falling) vs. US inequality (rising) vs. China inequality (rising).
image.caption: Figure 14.4.7: Globalization has reduced global poverty but increased inequality within many countries. This is the central tension in the globalization debate.

principles.principle: The Solution to Globalization's Costs Is Not Protectionism but Better Redistribution and Adjustment Assistance
principles.statement: Protectionism (tariffs, quotas, trade barriers) would reduce the gains from trade, harming consumers and exporters. The correct response to globalization's costs is not to stop globalization but to redistribute the gains and help workers adjust. This means: (1) strengthen social safety nets (unemployment insurance, health care, pensions); (2) invest in education and training; (3) provide adjustment assistance (retraining, relocation, wage insurance); (4) use progressive taxation to fund these programs; (5) include labor and environmental standards in trade agreements. The US and Europe have underinvested in these policies, leading to backlash against globalization. Countries that have invested (Denmark, Germany, Sweden) have maintained support for open trade.
principles.application: Governments should expand adjustment assistance programs, invest in education and training, and strengthen social safety nets. Trade agreements should include enforceable labor and environmental standards. The goal is to make globalization work for everyone, not just the winners. For Canada, this means maintaining open trade (USMCA, CETA, CPTPP) while strengthening EI, retraining programs, and social services.
principles.example: Denmark has high trade openness (exports+imports/GDP = 100%) but also a generous welfare state (flexicurity model). Workers who lose jobs receive high unemployment benefits, retraining, and job placement services. As a result, Denmark has maintained public support for free trade. The US, with weaker social safety nets, has seen backlash against globalization.

image.alt: Diagram: The flexicurity model (flexible labor markets + generous social safety nets + active labor market policies).
image.caption: Figure 14.4.8: The 'flexicurity' model (Denmark, Sweden) combines flexible labor markets with generous social safety nets and active labor market policies. This maintains support for globalization while protecting workers.

worked_examples.title: Calculating the Impact of Trade on Consumer Prices

image.alt: Table: Apparel prices before and after trade liberalization; calculations of price reduction and consumer savings.
image.caption: Figure 14.4.9: Worked example: calculating the impact of trade liberalization on consumer prices and savings.

worked_examples.problem:
Before trade liberalization, a country produced apparel domestically at high cost. After trade liberalization, apparel is imported from lower-cost countries. Data:

• Pre-liberalization price of a shirt: \$30
• Post-liberalization price: \$15
• Annual consumption of shirts: 100 million

a) Calculate the price reduction per shirt.
b) Calculate the total consumer savings per year.
c) If the country has 100 million households, what is the average savings per household?
d) Suppose 1 million apparel workers lose their jobs, each earning \$30,000 per year. What is the total wage loss?
e) Compare the consumer gains to the worker losses. Is there a net gain? What policy would you recommend?

step_by_step.explanation: Savings = Price reduction × Quantity = \$15 × 100,000,000 = \$1.5 billion per year

Savings per household = \$1.5 billion / 100 million = \$15 per household per year

This is a small amount per household, which explains why consumers do not mobilize to support free trade (diffuse benefits).

step_by_step.step: Total wage loss from job displacement
step_by_step.explanation: Wage loss = 1 million workers × \$30,000 = \$30 billion per year

Consumer gains: \$1.5 billion per year.
Worker losses: \$30 billion per year.

The losses appear much larger than the gains. However, this comparison is misleading because:

1. The \$30 billion is the total wages of displaced workers, not the net loss. Displaced workers may find new jobs (perhaps at lower wages). The net loss is the wage differential (old wage - new wage).
2. The consumer gains are only for shirts; there are gains for many other products (food, electronics, etc.).
3. There are also gains from increased variety and quality.
4. There are gains from productivity growth (resources reallocated to more efficient uses).

Most studies find that the net gains from trade are positive, but the gains are widely distributed (consumers) while the losses are concentrated (workers in specific industries). This is why trade is politically contentious: the losers are visible, the winners are invisible.

Policy recommendation: Provide adjustment assistance to displaced workers (retraining, unemployment benefits, wage insurance). The cost of adjustment assistance (say \$10,000 per worker for 1 million workers = \$10 billion) is less than the consumer gains (\$1.5 billion per year, but over many years). The government can use some of the gains from trade to fund adjustment assistance.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) \$15 per shirt. b) \$1.5 billion per year. c) \$15 per household. d) \$30 billion per year. e) Net gains positive but concentrated; policy: adjustment assistance for displaced workers.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying cost-benefit analysis to trade liberalization, comparing consumer gains to worker losses, and evaluating policy responses.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing the Impact of Offshoring on Wages

image.alt: Table: Wage changes for skilled and unskilled workers in developed and developing countries; calculations of wage inequality.
image.caption: Figure 14.4.10: Worked example: analyzing the impact of offshoring on wages for skilled and unskilled workers in developed and developing countries.

worked_examples.problem:
A developed country (US) and a developing country (China) trade. Before offshoring, both countries produce both goods. After offshoring, the US specializes in skilled-intensive goods (software, R&D), and China specializes in unskilled-intensive goods (manufacturing, assembly).

Wage changes:
• US skilled workers: wage up from \$50,000 to \$60,000.
• US unskilled workers: wage down from \$30,000 to \$25,000.
• China skilled workers: wage up from \$5,000 to \$10,000.
• China unskilled workers: wage up from \$2,000 to \$4,000.

a) Calculate the percentage change in wages for each group.
b) Which groups gained? Which lost?
c) Calculate the change in wage inequality in the US (ratio of skilled to unskilled wages).
d) Calculate the change in wage inequality in China.
e) Explain why offshoring increases inequality in developed countries but reduces inequality in developing countries (Stolper-Samuelson theorem).

step_by_step.explanation:
Winners: US skilled workers, China skilled workers, China unskilled workers.
Losers: US unskilled workers.

US unskilled workers lose because they compete with Chinese unskilled workers (import competition). China unskilled workers gain because they have a comparative advantage in unskilled-intensive goods (export expansion).

Initial ratio (skilled/unskilled) = 50,000 / 30,000 = 1.67
Final ratio = 60,000 / 25,000 = 2.40

Wage inequality increased in the US (from 1.67 to 2.40).

Initial ratio = 5,000 / 2,000 = 2.50
Final ratio = 10,000 / 4,000 = 2.50

Wage inequality in China remained constant (both skilled and unskilled wages doubled). In reality, inequality often increases in developing countries as well, but the Stolper-Samuelson theorem predicts that the abundant factor (unskilled labor) gains relative to the scarce factor (skilled labor) in developing countries.

The Stolper-Samuelson theorem states that trade benefits the owners of a country's abundant factors and harms the owners of scarce factors. In the US (developed, capital/skill abundant), skilled workers (abundant factor) gain; unskilled workers (scarce factor) lose. In China (developing, labor abundant), unskilled workers (abundant factor) gain; skilled workers (scarce factor) may lose or gain less. This explains why offshoring increases inequality in developed countries but can reduce inequality in developing countries (if unskilled workers are the abundant factor).

In this example, both skilled and unskilled workers in China gained, but if China had a scarcity of skilled workers, skilled workers might have gained less than unskilled workers, reducing inequality. In reality, China's inequality has increased, suggesting that other factors (urban-rural divide, hukou system) dominate the Stolper-Samuelson effect.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) US skilled: +20%, US unskilled: -16.7%, China skilled: +100%, China unskilled: +100%. b) Winners: US skilled, China skilled, China unskilled; Losers: US unskilled. c) US inequality increased from 1.67 to 2.40. d) China inequality unchanged (2.50 to 2.50). e) Stolper-Samuelson: trade benefits abundant factors, harms scarce factors.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the Stolper-Samuelson theorem to analyze the impact of offshoring on wages and inequality in developed and developing countries.

process_flow.process: Identify the dimensions of globalization (trade, investment, migration, technology)
process_flow.description: Assess the extent of integration in each dimension for the country or region of interest.
process_flow.mechanism: Trade openness (exports+imports/GDP); FDI stocks; migration rates; internet penetration.

image.alt: Diagram: Dimensions of globalization (trade, investment, migration, technology).
image.caption: Figure 14.4.11: Step 1 - Identify the dimensions of globalization (trade, investment, migration, technology) relevant to the analysis.

process_flow.process: Assess benefits (growth, poverty, prices, technology, peace)
process_flow.description: Measure the benefits of globalization using economic data (GDP growth, poverty rates, consumer prices, technology indicators, conflict data).
process_flow.mechanism: World Bank poverty data; CPI data; R&D spending; peace indices.

image.alt: Diagram: Benefits assessment (growth, poverty, prices, technology, peace).
image.caption: Figure 14.4.12: Step 2 - Assess the benefits of globalization using economic and social indicators.

process_flow.process: Assess costs (inequality, job loss, exploitation, environment, culture)
process_flow.description: Measure the costs of globalization using data on inequality (Gini coefficient), employment, working conditions, environmental indicators, cultural diversity.
process_flow.mechanism: Gini coefficient from World Bank; manufacturing employment data; pollution data; language diversity indices.

image.alt: Diagram: Costs assessment (inequality, job loss, exploitation, environment, culture).
image.caption: Figure 14.4.13: Step 3 - Assess the costs of globalization using inequality, employment, and environmental data.

process_flow.description: Determine which groups benefit and which groups lose from globalization (skilled vs. unskilled, capital vs. labor, developed vs. developing).
process_flow.mechanism: Stolper-Samuelson theorem; wage data by education level; sectoral employment data.

image.alt: Diagram: Winners (consumers, skilled workers, capital) and losers (unskilled workers, import-competing sectors).
image.caption: Figure 14.4.14: Step 4 - Identify the winners and losers from globalization using Stolper-Samuelson analysis and wage data.

process_flow.process: Design policy responses (adjustment assistance, education, redistribution, standards)
process_flow.description: Develop policies to manage the costs of globalization and redistribute the gains.
process_flow.mechanism: Adjustment assistance (retraining, UI), education spending, progressive taxation, labor/environmental standards in trade agreements.

image.alt: Diagram: Policy responses (adjustment assistance, education, redistribution, standards).
image.caption: Figure 14.4.15: Step 5 - Design policy responses to manage the costs of globalization and redistribute the gains.

image.alt: Master framework: Identify dimensions → assess benefits → assess costs → identify winners/losers → design policy responses.
image.caption: Figure 14.4.16: Complete framework for analyzing the globalization debate.

summary: Globalization—the integration of economies through trade, investment, capital flows, migration, and technology—has generated significant benefits but also significant costs. Benefits include economic growth, poverty reduction (global poverty rate fell from 35% to 9% between 1990 and 2020), lower consumer prices, technology transfer, economies of scale, and peace. Costs include rising inequality within many countries, job displacement (US manufacturing employment fell from 17 million to 12 million), worker exploitation (sweatshops), environmental damage, cultural homogenization, financial instability, and pandemic risk. The winners from globalization are consumers, skilled workers in developed countries, unskilled workers in developing countries, multinational corporations, and capital owners. The losers are unskilled workers in developed countries (manufacturing job losses) and some workers in import-competing sectors. The Stolper-Samuelson theorem explains this pattern: trade benefits abundant factors and harms scarce factors. The policy response should not be protectionism (which would reduce the gains from trade) but rather redistribution and adjustment assistance: strengthen social safety nets, invest in education and training, provide retraining and wage insurance, use progressive taxation to fund these programs, and include labor and environmental standards in trade agreements. The 'flexicurity' model (Denmark, Germany) combines flexible labor markets with generous social safety nets, maintaining support for globalization. For Canada, globalization has been a net benefit, but policies to support displaced workers are essential. Understanding the globalization debate is essential for evaluating trade agreements, assessing the impact of offshoring, and designing policies to make globalization inclusive.
